topics.titleEn: Kubernetes Autoscaling: HPA, VPA, and CA
topics.angle: guide + combining HPA, VPA, and Cluster Autoscaler without them fighting each other
topics.brief: Sections: HPA (horizontal, by metric), VPA (right-sizing requests), Cluster Autoscaler (node scaling), and why HPA+VPA on the same resource/metric conflict; custom and external metrics; resource quotas for fairness; cost impact. Key claims to verify: 86% of cloud environments lack HPA, quietly inflating spend; combining the three is the recommended data-driven approach. Primary sources: KodeKloud Kubernetes best practices; ScaleOps/Spot cost-optimization 2026. End with a config matrix and the HPA/VPA conflict workaround.

topics.titleEn: Secrets Management in the Cloud, Done Right
topics.angle: how-to + moving off .env files to managed secrets with rotation and least privilege
topics.brief: Contrarian: your .env file is a breach waiting to happen. Sections: why env files and committed secrets fail; managed options (cloud secret managers, Vault, SOPS-encrypted git); short-lived dynamic credentials; automatic rotation; scoping and audit. Key claims to verify: dynamic short-lived credentials beat long-lived keys; SOPS lets you keep encrypted secrets in git safely. Primary sources: HashiCorp Vault docs; Mozilla SOPS. End with a migration path from .env to a secret manager and a rotation checklist. No real secrets in examples.

topics.titleEn: Blue-Green vs Canary Deployments
topics.angle: comparison + rollout strategies, rollback speed, and cost tradeoffs
topics.brief: Question-led: which rollout fits your risk tolerance? Sections: blue-green (two full environments, instant switch, instant rollback, double cost); canary (gradual traffic shift, metric gates, cheaper, slower); rolling as the baseline; database-change coupling and feature-flag pairing. Key claims to verify: blue-green gives fastest rollback but doubles infra; canary limits blast radius via progressive traffic. Primary sources: Google/GitLab deployment-strategy docs; CNCF progressive-delivery (Argo Rollouts/Flagger). End with a decision table by team maturity and workload.

topics.titleEn: Postgres Connection Pooling with PgBouncer
topics.angle: how-to + pool modes, sizing, and the RLS/session footguns behind a pooler
topics.brief: Contrarian: pooling silently breaks your tenant isolation. Sections: why Postgres connections are expensive; PgBouncer multiplexing (e.g. 500 app to 20 DB connections); pool modes (session/transaction/statement) and what each disables; the RLS footgun (shared role makes current_user useless behind a pooler); fix with SET LOCAL in a transaction. Key claims to verify: transaction pooling breaks session features; SET LOCAL resets tenant context safely. Primary sources: Bytebase Postgres RLS footguns; PgBouncer docs. Note PgBouncer 1.25.2 CVE-2026-6664 patch. End with sizing guidance.

topics.titleEn: The Staff Engineer Path: Beyond Senior
topics.angle: guide + scope, influence, and the writing habit that gets you promoted
topics.brief: Story of a senior who plateaued until they started writing. Sections: what Staff means (technical leadership without reports, cross-team multiplier); archetypes (tech lead, architect, solver, right hand); how to demonstrate scope; sponsorship vs mentorship; the RFC/design-doc habit as highest-leverage signal. Key claims to verify: writing design docs and RFCs is the single highest-leverage skill for Staff promotion; Staff total comp ~$400K-$800K at top firms. Primary sources: jobsbyculture Staff Engineer 2026; StaffEng.com. End with a 90-day plan to build a promotion case.

topics.titleEn: How to Write an Engineering RFC
topics.angle: how-to + a reusable template, tradeoff framing, and a review flow that converges
topics.brief: Field notes on RFCs that shipped vs stalled. Sections: when an RFC is worth it; a template (context, goals/non-goals, options with tradeoffs, decision, risks, rollout); writing for skimmers; running async review and closing debate; recording the decision. Key claims to verify: clear written technical communication is the top Staff-track skill; documented tradeoffs speed alignment. Primary sources: jobsbyculture Staff Engineer 2026; Oxide/Rust RFC process. End with a fill-in template and anti-patterns (solution-first, no non-goals, unbounded scope).

topics.titleEn: Salary Negotiation for Developers in 2026
topics.angle: guide + using impact metrics and comp bands as leverage
topics.brief: Sections: researching bands (levels data, transparency laws); why you let them name a range first; building an impact dossier (latency, error rate, cost per request, revenue); the AI-skills wage premium; competing offers and total-comp components. Key claims to verify: AI-skilled workers earn a 56% wage premium (PwC 2025, up from 25%); documented production metrics strengthen negotiation; ask for the range instead of stating a number. Primary sources: HackerX tech salary guide 2026; PwC Global AI Jobs Barometer. End with a metrics template and scripts.

topics.titleEn: Building an AI-Proof Developer Skill Set
topics.angle: contrarian + what stays valuable when AI writes most of the code
topics.brief: Contrarian: chasing the newest framework is a losing bet; judgment isn't. Sections: skills that compound (system design, debugging, reviewing AI output, writing, product sense); the risk of over- or under-using AI; proving impact with metrics. Key claims to verify: recruiters value architect-level thinking and clear communication over framework mastery; the productive middle path is thoughtful AI integration, not rejection or blind acceptance. Primary sources: Rootstack tech skills 2026; zenvanriel AI career pathways 2026. End with a self-audit and a learning plan weighted to durable skills.

topics.titleEn: How to Run Effective Code Reviews
topics.angle: guide + scope, tone, speed, and reviewing AI-generated pull requests
topics.brief: Contrarian: nitpicking style wastes everyone's time; review for risk. Sections: what review is for (correctness, design, maintainability, not formatting a linter handles); small PRs and fast turnaround; tone and blocking vs non-blocking comments; author responsibilities; reviewing AI-generated PRs skeptically. Key claims to verify: fast, focused reviews reduce cycle time; AI-written PRs need extra scrutiny as AI slop rises. Primary sources: Google engineering code-review guidelines; SmartBear/industry review data. End with a reviewer checklist and a comment-severity convention.

topics.titleEn: Building in Public: A Developer's Playbook
topics.angle: guide + audience, cadence, and what to share without oversharing
topics.brief: Question-led: does building in public actually help a dev career? Sections: why it compounds (network, hiring signal, feedback); what to share (progress, learnings, metrics) vs what to keep private (secrets, unshipped strategy); cadence and platform choice; turning a build log into a portfolio. Key claims to verify: visible work and clear communication are hiring signals in 2026; documenting impact aids promotion and negotiation. Primary sources: Rootstack tech skills 2026; indie-hacker build-in-public case studies. End with a lightweight weekly-sharing routine.

topics.titleTr: Bun mu Node.js mi: 2026 Runtime Kararı
topics.titleEn: Bun vs Node.js: The 2026 Runtime Verdict
topics.angle: comparison + raw benchmarks vs real-world latency and migration risk
topics.brief: Data-led: benchmarks vs reality. Sections: raw HTTP throughput (Bun ~52K req/s vs Node ~13K) collapses to ~3% once Postgres and serialization enter; cold starts (Bun 8-15ms vs Node 40-120ms) matter for serverless; install speed (~35x npm); native TS, built-in test runner, and native-addon/ecosystem gaps. Key claims to verify: the ~4x synthetic gap shrinks to ~3% in DB-bound apps; Bun cold starts ~4.6x faster; migrating a mature Node app is risk with limited upside. Primary sources: Strapi Bun-vs-Node 2026 benchmarks; daily.dev runtime comparison. End with a greenfield-vs-migrate decision rule.

topics.titleEn: Migrating to Tailwind CSS v4
topics.angle: how-to + upgrading a real project to the CSS-first v4 engine
topics.brief: Field notes from upgrading a real project. Sections: CSS-first @theme config replacing tailwind.config.js; the Rust/Oxide engine and 60-80% faster cold builds; renamed utilities (bg-gradient-to-* to bg-linear-to-*, shrink-0); OKLCH default palette; native @layer cascade; running the @tailwindcss/upgrade codemod. Key claims to verify: v4 drops the PostCSS plugin and JS config; the codemod handles ~90% of mechanical changes; v4 targets Safari 16.4+/Chrome 111+/Firefox 128+. Primary sources: Tailwind official upgrade guide; LogRocket Tailwind 2026 guide. End with a migration checklist and a rollback note for older-browser targets.

topics.titleTr: Modern CSS: :has() ve Yerel İç İçe Yazım
topics.titleEn: Modern CSS: :has() and Native Nesting
topics.angle: how-to + the CSS features that let you delete JavaScript and preprocessors
topics.brief: Field notes: CSS that deleted our JavaScript. Sections: :has() as the long-awaited parent selector (style a card by its contents, drive form-validation states); native nesting replacing preprocessors; pairing with container queries; progressive-enhancement fallbacks with @supports. Key claims to verify: :has() and native nesting are fully supported across modern browsers in 2026; :has() replaces class-toggling JS; native nesting removes a key reason teams kept Sass. Primary sources: MDN CSS nesting docs; caniuse :has()/nesting support tables. End with three copy-paste patterns and a browser-support caveat for older targets.

topics.titleTr: SSE mi WebSocket mi: Gerçek Zamanlı Veri
topics.titleEn: SSE vs WebSockets for Real-Time Apps
topics.angle: comparison + choosing the simplest transport that fits your data flow
topics.brief: Question-led: do you really need WebSockets? Sections: SSE (unidirectional, over HTTP, auto-reconnect, HTTP/2 multiplexing) vs WebSockets (full-duplex, bidirectional); why LLM token streaming uses SSE; firewall/proxy behavior; when bidirectional truly matters (chat, collaboration, multiplayer). Key claims to verify: SSE fits ~90% of dashboards/notifications and is simpler to run; OpenAI/Anthropic stream responses via SSE; WebSockets require you to write reconnection yourself. Primary sources: Ably WebSockets-vs-SSE 2026; MDN Server-Sent Events. End with a decision table and a minimal SSE endpoint plus HTTP POST for the return path.

topics.titleEn: React Compiler: Goodbye useMemo?
topics.angle: deep-dive + what auto-memoization changes and the escape hatches that remain
topics.brief: Story of a codebase drowning in useMemo. Sections: what React Compiler auto-memoizes at build time; why manual useMemo/useCallback/React.memo become unnecessary in ~95% of cases; the surviving escape hatches (interior-mutability libraries like react-hook-form watch, effect dependencies); safe rollout (don't mass-delete existing memoization). Key claims to verify: React Compiler 1.0 shipped stable in Oct 2025; it memoizes more precisely than hand-tuning; useMemo remains an escape hatch, not a default. Primary sources: react.dev React Compiler docs; React Compiler 1.0 migration guides. End with an adoption checklist and lint-rule setup.

topics.titleEn: Caching Strategies and Invalidation
topics.angle: guide + the four patterns, TTL discipline, and surviving a stampede
topics.brief: Field notes on caches that lied. Sections: cache-aside (lazy) vs read-through vs write-through vs write-behind and their consistency tradeoffs; invalidation (delete-on-write plus TTL); TTL guidance by data type; the stampede/thundering-herd failure and fixes (mutex/single-flight, probabilistic early expiration). Key claims to verify: cache-aside is the common default; combining TTL with event-driven invalidation avoids stale reads; a stampede on one hot key can overwhelm a DB that was fine with the cache. Primary sources: system-design caching-strategies references; oneuptime cache-invalidation 2026. End with a per-workload TTL table and a stampede-mitigation snippet.

topics.titleTr: Cursor mu Offset mi: API Sayfalama
topics.titleEn: Cursor vs Offset Pagination Explained
topics.angle: comparison + why deep pages are slow and how keyset fixes correctness
topics.brief: Data-led: why page 5000 is slow. Sections: offset (LIMIT/OFFSET scans and discards rows) vs keyset/cursor (WHERE (col,id) > (...) ORDER BY col,id); the correctness bug where deletes shift rows across pages so users skip or duplicate records; a ~17x speedup at a million rows; when offset is fine (small/static admin data). Key claims to verify: OFFSET 99980 scans ~100k rows before returning 20; keyset avoids skipped/duplicated rows during inserts and deletes; ~17x faster at 1M rows in Postgres. Primary sources: Sequin keyset-cursors-not-offsets post; Gusto API-pagination guide. End with a keyset query template and a cursor-encoding note.

topics.titleEn: Database Isolation Levels, Explained
topics.angle: deep-dive + which level you actually need and the retries it forces
topics.brief: Question-led: which isolation level do you actually need? Sections: read anomalies (dirty read, non-repeatable read, phantom, lost update); the four SQL levels (read uncommitted to serializable); what Postgres vs MySQL default to; MVCC and the serialization failures you must retry. Key claims to verify: Postgres defaults to Read Committed while many assume higher; Postgres Repeatable Read uses snapshot isolation and prevents phantoms; Serializable can abort transactions and needs retry logic. Primary sources: PostgreSQL transaction-isolation docs; Kleppmann 'Designing Data-Intensive Applications' isolation chapter. End with a level-vs-anomaly matrix and a retry pattern.

topics.titleEn: The Transactional Outbox Pattern
topics.angle: deep-dive + publishing events atomically with your database write
topics.brief: Story of a lost payment event. Sections: the dual-write problem (DB commit succeeds, broker publish fails, or vice versa); writing events to an outbox table inside the same transaction as the business change; a relay that publishes them (polling vs log-based CDC with Debezium); idempotent consumers under at-least-once delivery. Key claims to verify: you cannot atomically write a DB and publish to a broker without a pattern like this; log-based CDC gives low-latency relay with no extra DB load; consumers must dedupe. Primary sources: microservices.io transactional-outbox; Debezium outbox-event-router docs. End with a schema and a relay checklist.

topics.titleEn: Chunking Strategies for Better RAG
topics.angle: how-to + picking a chunking method and defaults that actually retrieve
topics.brief: Data-led playbook. Sections: fixed vs recursive vs semantic vs agentic chunking; recommended defaults (256-512 tokens, 10-20% overlap); recursive 512-token splitting as a strong 2026 baseline; semantic chunking for unstructured prose; late chunking with long-context embeddings. Key claims to verify: a Feb 2026 benchmark ranked recursive 512-token splitting first across seven strategies; semantic chunking lifted accuracy to ~71% vs fixed-size baselines on one dataset; agentic chunking is highest quality but slow and expensive. Primary sources: Firecrawl best-chunking-strategies 2026; Extend semantic-chunking best practices. End with a strategy-selection flowchart and an eval harness note.

topics.titleTr: Küçük Dil Modelleri Ne Zaman Yeter?
topics.titleEn: When Small Language Models Beat Big Ones
topics.angle: contrarian + you are overpaying for a frontier model on narrow tasks
topics.brief: Contrarian: you're overpaying for a frontier model. Sections: SLMs (a few million to ~7B params) win on cost, latency, privacy, and on-device deploy; where a fine-tuned 7B beats a generalist on narrow, repeatable tasks; where big models stay necessary (broad knowledge, open-ended reasoning); the routing pattern (a small model handles most requests and escalates hard cases). Key claims to verify: fine-tuned SLMs are faster/cheaper/more accurate on repeatable workloads; routing plus drafting is the 2026 best practice; frontier models remain needed for unpredictable reasoning. Primary sources: index.dev SLM-vs-LLM 2026 reality check; MachineLearningMastery SLM guide. End with a build/route decision checklist.

topics.titleEn: LLM Observability: Tracing Your AI Calls
topics.angle: how-to + instrumenting an LLM app with OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions
topics.brief: Field notes: debugging an agent you can't see. Sections: why request/response logging isn't enough (chains, tool calls, retries); OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions (gen_ai.* attributes for model, tokens, tool calls, finish reason); tracing with Langfuse as an OTel/OTLP backend; wiring evals, cost, and latency dashboards. Key claims to verify: the field converged on OTel gen_ai.* as the vendor-neutral baseline; Langfuse ingests OTLP and self-hosts for data residency (now part of ClickHouse); token/cost/finish-reason attributes are standardized. Primary sources: opentelemetry.io GenAI-observability 2026 blog; Langfuse OpenTelemetry docs. End with a minimal instrumentation snippet.

topics.titleEn: From IC to Tech Lead: The First 90 Days
topics.angle: guide + a 30-60-90 plan for the shift from writing code to leading
topics.brief: Story of a strong IC's rocky first quarter. Sections: the identity shift (impact through others, not commits); a 30-60-90 plan (listen and map, then voice observations around week 4-6, then lead changes); building a wide network (skip-level, peer leads, cross-functional partners); letting go of being the best coder. Key claims to verify: coding stops being the primary job and this surprises most new leads; the shift from listening to leading lands around weeks 4-6; first-90-days relationships become a multi-year network. Primary sources: LeadDev 30-60-90 new-manager plan; First Round 90-day plan. End with a fill-in 30-60-90 template.

topics.titleEn: How to Make Better Engineering Estimates
topics.angle: how-to + decomposition, ranges over point estimates, and calibration
topics.brief: Field notes on estimates that blew up. Sections: why estimates fail (unstated assumptions, ignored unknowns, optimism bias); relative sizing vs hours; ranges and confidence over single numbers; breaking work down until each item is boring; tracking estimate-vs-actual to calibrate over time. Key claims to verify: decomposition reduces estimation error; communicating a range beats false-precision point estimates; historical calibration improves forecasts more than heroics. Primary sources: Steve McConnell 'Software Estimation: Demystifying the Black Art'; team retrospectives on estimate accuracy. End with an estimation checklist and a phrase-bank for pushing back on false precision.

topics.titleEn: Software Supply Chain Security: SBOM to SLSA
topics.angle: checklist + SBOMs, signing, and build provenance wired into CI
topics.brief: Question-led: could a poisoned dependency reach your prod? Sections: the threat (cumulative malicious packages past 1.2M; attacks doubled in 2025); SBOMs enriched with VEX instead of paper inventories; artifact signing with Sigstore (cosign, Fulcio, Rekor); build provenance via SLSA; wiring these checks into CI. Key claims to verify: Sonatype counted 450k+ new malicious packages in 2025; Sigstore's cosign/Fulcio/Rekor are production-ready and widely adopted; SLSA provides a verifiable source-to-artifact chain of custody. Primary sources: Cloudsmith 2026 supply-chain guide; OpenSSF Sigstore docs. End with a CI hardening checklist. No real secrets in examples.

topics.titleTr: Helm mi Kustomize mi: K8s Yapılandırma
topics.titleEn: Helm vs Kustomize for Kubernetes Config
topics.angle: comparison + templating vs overlays and where mature teams land
topics.brief: Data-led comparison for real fleets. Sections: Helm (versioned charts, Go templating, releases, rollbacks, huge chart ecosystem) vs Kustomize (base plus overlays, no templating, built into kubectl); Helm 4 (server-side apply, WASM plugins) and Kustomize v5.8 interop; the common convergence (Kustomize for first-party apps, Helm for third-party charts). Key claims to verify: Helm 4 shipped at KubeCon NA Nov 2025 with native SSA and a WASM plugin system; Kustomize is built into kubectl and templating-free; mature teams mix both. Primary sources: Spacelift Kustomize-vs-Helm; Tasrie 'we manage 100+ clusters' 2026. End with a decision table and a hybrid setup.
